---
title: "Time Series and Trends"
subtitle: "Multiple series, annotation, smoothing, and the risks of connecting the dots"
author: "Jared Edgerton"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, metropolis, metropolis-fonts, plsc498.css]
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      slideNumberFormat: ""
---

class: center, middle, inverse, title-slide

.title[
# Time Series and Trends
]
.subtitle[
## Multiple series, annotation, smoothing, and the risks of connecting the dots
]
.author[
### Jared Edgerton
]

---

# Quiz on canvas

- Log on to the course website to take it.
- You have 10 minutes.
- It is open notes and web.
- Do not generate your answers with AI.

---

# Quick Recap

- Last time, we focused on **associations among quantitative variables**:
  - scatterplots and smoothers
  - correlation (Pearson vs rank-based)
  - correlograms
  - paired data and slopegraphs

Today, we focus on **time series**:

- line charts and their assumptions
- multiple time series (color vs facets)
- annotation (events, labels)
- smoothing and functional forms (rolling mean vs LOESS)
- seasonality vs trend
- when "connecting the dots" misleads

---

# Setup

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(scales)

set.seed(42)
```

---

# Simulated data for today

We simulate monthly data for three countries over 20 years. This gives us: (a) trends, (b) seasonality, (c) noise, and (d) a structural break (event).

```{r}
dates <- seq(as.Date("2003-01-01"), as.Date("2022-12-01"), by = "month")
n <- length(dates)

df <- expand.grid(date = dates, country = c("Aurelia", "Belvica", "Cordonia")) %>%
  arrange(country, date) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(
    t = row_number(),
    trend = case_when(
      country == "Aurelia" ~ 50 + 0.08 * t,
      country == "Belvica" ~ 55 - 0.03 * t,
      country == "Cordonia" ~ 45 + 0.05 * t + ifelse(t > 150, -8, 0)
    ),
    season = 3 * sin(2 * pi * t / 12),
    noise = rnorm(n(), 0, 2.5),
    approval = trend + season + noise,
    year = as.numeric(format(date, "%Y")),
    month = as.numeric(format(date, "%m"))
  ) %>%
  ungroup()
```

---

# Why time series plots are different

A time series plot is a claim that **order matters**.

When you connect points with a line, you assert:

- adjacent observations are meaningfully related
- the path between them is interpretable

This is different from a scatterplot, where x-y order is arbitrary.

**Core question:** is the x-axis (time) evenly spaced and complete?

---

# Baseline: a single time series

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
df_a <- df %>% filter(country == "Aurelia")

ggplot(df_a, aes(x = date, y = approval)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Aurelia: monthly approval rating")
```

---

# What can you see?

From this single line chart:

- **Trend:** is approval going up, down, or flat?
- **Seasonality:** are there regular cycles?
- **Noise:** how much month-to-month variation?
- **Events:** any sudden jumps or drops?

Always start by describing these four features.

---

# Multiple series: color

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = approval, color = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)", color = NULL,
       title = "Three countries on one panel")
```

Interpretation:
- good for **a few series** with different levels
- gets messy when lines cross frequently

---

# Multiple series: small multiples

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = approval)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 1) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Same data, faceted for clarity")
```

Interpretation:
- easier to see each country's pattern
- harder to compare exact levels

---

# Pause and discuss with a neighbor

You have two time series designs:

1) overlaid colored lines
2) faceted panels

**Prompt:**
- Which one would you choose for a policy brief?
- What question is each one better at answering?

---

# Data visualization critique

- Take five minutes
  - What variables are mapped to what (x/y/color/size/etc.)?
  - Is it persuasive?
  - What design choices did they make?
  - After doing this talk to your neighbor briefly and compare notes.

.center[
<img src="airline_preference.png" style="width:50%;">
]

---

# Annotation: marking events

Good time series plots often need context. Annotation = text, lines, or shading that marks when something happened.

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
ggplot(df %>% filter(country == "Cordonia"), aes(x = date, y = approval)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2015-07-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = as.Date("2015-10-01"), y = 65,
           label = "Policy reform", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Cordonia: approval with event annotation")
```

---

# Annotation principles

When annotating time series:

- **Label the event**, not just the date
- Use vertical lines or shaded regions for periods
- Keep annotation text short (2--4 words)
- Place labels where they don't overlap the data

**Rule:**
If the reader has to guess what happened at a date, your annotation is missing.

---

# Data visualization critique

- Take five minutes
  - What variables are mapped to what (x/y/color/size/etc.)?
  - Is it persuasive?
  - What design choices did they make?
  - After doing this talk to your neighbor briefly and compare notes.

.center[
<img src="coconut_pinneapple.png" style="width:50%;">
]

---

# Smoothing: separating signal from noise

Raw time series are noisy. Smoothing helps the reader focus on the trend.

Two common choices:

- **Rolling mean:** average of the last *k* observations
- **LOESS:** locally weighted regression (ggplot default smoother)

Both are modeling decisions. The reader sees your choice of smoothness.

---

# Rolling mean (manual)

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
df_a <- df_a %>%
  arrange(date) %>%
  mutate(roll_12 = zoo::rollmean(approval, k = 12, fill = NA, align = "right"))

ggplot(df_a, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = approval), alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_line(aes(y = roll_12), color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Aurelia: raw data + 12-month rolling mean")
```

---

# LOESS smoother (ggplot default)

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
ggplot(df_a, aes(x = date, y = approval)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1, color = "firebrick") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Aurelia: raw data + LOESS smoother")
```

Interpretation:
- LOESS adapts to local patterns
- can pick up curvature that a rolling mean smooths away

---

# Smoothing comparison: what changes

- **Too smooth:** hides real events (e.g., a crisis)
- **Too wiggly:** makes noise look like events
- **Rolling mean:** transparent (you know it averages *k* points)
- **LOESS:** flexible but harder to explain

**Practical habit:**
Try at least two smoothing levels. If the story changes, report both.

---

# Seasonality vs trend

Many time series have both:

- a **trend** (long-run direction)
- **seasonality** (regular, repeating cycles)

Confusing these leads to bad conclusions:

- "Approval is spiking!" (no, it does that every January)
- "The trend reversed!" (no, you're seeing winter vs summer)

---

# Highlighting seasonality

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
df_season <- df %>%
  filter(country == "Aurelia") %>%
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarise(mean_approval = mean(approval), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(df_season, aes(x = factor(month), y = mean_approval, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = "Month", y = "Mean approval (%)",
       title = "Aurelia: seasonal pattern (averaged across years)")
```

---

# Data visualization critique

- Take five minutes
  - What variables are mapped to what (x/y/color/size/etc.)?
  - Is it persuasive?
  - What design choices did they make?
  - After doing this talk to your neighbor briefly and compare notes.

.center[
<img src="mother_partner_age.png" style="width:50%;">
]

---

# When connecting the dots misleads

A line chart assumes evenly spaced, complete data.

If measurements are irregular:
- the line implies interpolation that may be wrong
- slopes can be misleading (steeper = faster, but the gap might be longer)

**Rule:**
If your time axis has gaps, show points. Only connect when spacing is regular.

---

# Example: irregular spacing

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
set.seed(99)
irreg <- data.frame(
  date = sort(sample(seq(as.Date("2010-01-01"), as.Date("2020-12-31"), by = "month"), 30)),
  value = cumsum(rnorm(30, 0.2, 1.5)) + 50
)

ggplot(irreg, aes(x = date, y = value)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Value",
       title = "Irregular spacing: line can mislead about rate of change")
```

---

# Axis formatting for dates

Good time series labeling:

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
ggplot(df %>% filter(country == "Aurelia"), aes(x = date, y = approval)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "2 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Approval (%)",
       title = "Formatted x-axis with scale_x_date()")
```

---

# What success looks like

- You can identify **trend**, **seasonality**, **noise**, and **events** in a time series
- You can choose between overlaid lines and facets deliberately
- You annotate events with text and reference lines
- You can explain your smoothing choice (rolling mean vs LOESS) and its implications
- You recognize when connecting dots is misleading (irregular spacing)

---

# In-Class Activity

Find a visualization online.

With a neighbor:
- Identify the data variables
- Identify the visual mappings
- Decide what message the plot is making
- Suggest one concrete improvement

---

# What Comes Next

Next, we will practice these patterns in lab:

- line charts with real data
- annotation and labeling
- smoothing choices (rolling mean vs LOESS)
- uncertainty ribbons as a preview of Week 14
- clear axis formatting for time data

Focus on clarity, not complexity.
